mappings.rationaleAI: Aggregate economic analysis covering classical versus Keynesian theories of demand and unemployment (A.1), circular flow of income (A.2), fluctuations in national income (A.3), international equilibrium (B), and business cycles (C).
Social science encompassing income and employment theory (A), international economic and financial equilibrium (B), and business cycle analysis (C).
Financial intermediary relevant to foreign exchange market operations (B.1) and international monetary institutions (B.2).
Aggregate supply-aggregate demand model relevant to the classical law of markets versus Keynesian theory of effective demand (A.1.a).
Monetary resource management covering foreign exchange markets (B.1) and international monetary institutions (B.2).
Medium of exchange relevant to foreign exchange markets and monetary standards (B.1) and problems of maintaining adequate gold and currency reserves (B.2.a).
Classical political economy formulating the law of markets (Say's Law) relevant to the classical theory contrasted with Keynesian demand (A.1.a).
Government fiscal instrument relevant to countercyclical fiscal policy (C.3).
Severe downturn illustrating unemployment theory debates (A.1.b), fluctuations in national income (A.3), economic aftermath of major crises (B.2.b), and business cycle analysis (C).
Market economy system relevant to income and employment theory (A) and the occurrence of business cycles within capitalist economies (C).
Supranational union with Eurozone and coordinated monetary policy, constituting an international monetary institution and attempt at cooperation (B.2).
Government-funded support systems functioning as countercyclical fiscal policy through automatic stabilizers (C.3).
Noble metal with monetary history directly relevant to problems of maintaining adequate gold reserves, gold crises, and special drawing rights (B.2.a).
Transfer of goods and services relevant to balance of payments accounting (B.1.c).
Productive activity directly relevant to classical and Keynesian theories of unemployment (A.1.b).
Proto-macroeconomic analysis including supply-demand, labor theory of value, and taxation, anticipating cyclical theories of economic fluctuation (C.2).
Discipline of data analysis directly relevant to the statistical study of cycles, identification, and measurement of business cycles (C.1).
